```{r text3: animals, include=FALSE}
  summarise(
    # unweighted
    slaughter = sm(slaughter),
    disease = sm(disease),
    theft = sm(theft),
    injury = sm(injury),
    all_lost = sm(all_lost)
  ) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  setDT() %>% 
  # weighted hh equivalents that lost any animal to the categories listed
  weigh() %>% 
  mutate(across(c(slaughter:all_lost), 
               ~ ifelse(.x > 0, weight_adj, 0), .names = "w_{col}"))

sum(hhls$w_slaughter)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj)
sum(hhls$w_disease)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj)
sum(hhls$w_theft)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj)
sum(hhls$w_injury)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj)
sum(hhls$w_all_lost) # number of households with losses

aown
text3
hhls
```

An estimated `r round(sum(hhls$w_all_lost)/mio,1)` million households (`r round(sum(hhls$w_all_lost)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj),1)`% of livestock owning households) incurred livestock losses equivalent of `r round(sum(text3$w_total_lost)/mio,1)` million animals. The cause of loss that affected most households was disease (`r round(sum(hhls$w_disease)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj),1)`%), followed by injury (`r round(sum(hhls$w_injury)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj),1)`%) and `r round(sum(hhls$w_theft)*100/sum(lvstowners$weight_adj),1)`% of households were affected by theft. In terms of animals lost the most animals across livestock category died of disease, accounting for `r text3 %>% filter(type == "large ruminants") %>% pull(w_pdis)`% and `r text3 %>% filter(type == "small ruminants") %>% pull(w_pdis)`% among large and small ruminants and `r text3 %>% filter(type == "poultry") %>% pull(w_pdis)`% among poultry. Between `r text3 %>% filter(type == "large ruminants") %>% pull(w_pinjured)`% (large ruminants) and `r text3 %>% filter(type == "poultry") %>% pull(w_pdis)`% (poultry) were lost to injury. Theft was a relatively uncommon causes of loss ranging between `r text3 %>% filter(type == "large ruminants") %>% pull(w_pstolen)`% for large ruminants and `r text3 %>% filter(type == "pigs") %>% pull(w_pstolen)`% for pigs (Table \@ref(tab:animalloss)).

### Destinations: Storage, processing, sales and household consumption

```{r storage, include=FALSE}
storage <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "storage.csv")) %>% setDT()

storage[, `:=` (
  pstor = stored/produced, # % of harvest in storage
  pseed = seed/stored, # % of storage for seed
  n = ifelse(stored>0, 1, 0), # count of items in storage
  w_produced = produced*weight_adj,
  w_stored = stored*weight_adj,
  w_seed = seed*weight_adj,
  w_pstor = (stored*weight_adj)/(produced*weight_adj),
  w_pseed = (seed*weight_adj)/(stored*weight_adj))] 

# Overview of households storing, quantities in storage and proportion of which are seed, weighted - x
storage_stats <- storage %>% 
  filter(produced>0) %>% # contains only livestock owning households
  group_by(type, cropid) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted sample
    n_obs = n(), # producers
    n_stor = length(stored[stored>0]), # storers
    perc = n_stor/n_obs*100,   # perc of producers storing
    total = sm(stored),
    ptotal = sm(stored)/sm(produced)*100,
    totalseed = sm(seed),
    pseed = sm(seed)/sm(stored),
    # weighted sample
    neq = sum(weight_adj), # household equivalents storing
    w_total = sm(w_stored), # total quantity stored
    w_ptotal = sm(w_stored)/sm(w_produced)*100, # proportion stored
    w_totalseed = sm(w_seed), # total seed stored
    w_pseed = sm(w_seed)/sm(w_stored)*100 # proportion seed stored
  ) %>% 
  filter(ptotal>.1) %>% 
  arrange(desc(w_ptotal)) %>% 
  rd()

seed <- sm(storage_stats$w_totalseed)
```

```{r include=F}
storagemethods <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "storagemethods.csv"))

hhstor <- storagemethods %>% 
  
  mutate(storbin = ifelse(stored>0, 1,0)) %>% 
  select(y4_hhid, storbin, weight_adj) %>% 
  filter(storbin == 1) %>% 
  unique()

# Purpose of storage - TBD

# Overview of methods used, number of households and total quantities affected - x
stor_stat <- storagemethods %>% 
  group_by(method) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted
    n_obs = n(), # count of method-crop
    storage = sum(stored),
    pstor = storage*100/sum(storagemethods$stored), # percentage of total with method
    # weighted
    neq = sum(weight_adj), # not accurate as double counts households if used method multiple times
    w_storage = sum(w_stored),
    wpstor = w_storage*100/sum(storagemethods$w_stored)
  ) %>% 
  arrange(desc(pstor)) %>% 
  rd()
```

```{r include=FALSE}
processed <- produce_long %>% 
  filter(product_type == "Processed crop"| subtype == "Processed milk") %>%  # to be added: 
  group_by(type, subtype, item) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted
    n_obs = n(), # double counts households in food category
    sum = sum(produced), # total produce (not relative to overall production)
    # weighted
    w_sum = sum(w_produced),
    psold = sum(w_sold)*100/w_sum, # sold is given, consumed is estimated
    w_waste = sum(w_uncertain), # estimated waste; should not be 0 since new calculation
    neq = sm(weight_adj))
```

The largest volume of a single crop in storage was for maize (`r round(max(storage_stats$w_total)/mio,1)` Mt) representing `r storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "maize") %>% pull(w_ptotal)`% of total production, followed by sorghum (`r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "sorghum") %>% pull(w_total)/mio,1)` Mt, `r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "sorghum") %>% pull(ptotal),1)`%) and rice (`r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "paddy") %>% pull(w_total)/mio,1)` Mt, `r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "paddy") %>% pull(ptotal),1)`%). Proportionally, the largest share of any crop in storage was for wheat (`r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "wheat") %>% pull(ptotal),1)`%) and bulrush millet (`r round(storage_stats %>% filter(cropid == "bulrush millet") %>% pull(ptotal),1)`%). Seed for the next season made up `r round(seed/sm(storage_stats$w_total),1)`% of total quantities in storage. Most common storage methods were sack and open drums, a method used for `r max(stor_stat$wpstor)`% of storage volumes followed by locally made traditional structures (`r stor_stat %>% filter(grepl("traditional", method)) %>% pull(wpstor)`%). Only `r stor_stat %>% filter(method == "modern store") %>% pull(wpstor) + stor_stat %>% filter(method == "modern structure") %>% pull(wpstor)`% of crop was stored in modern storage or structures. Eight households (`r round(8/nrow(hhstor)*100,1)`% of households storing crops) reported to have used modern storage (equivalent to `r round((stor_stat %>% filter(method == "modern store") %>% pull(neq) + stor_stat %>% filter(method == "modern structure") %>% pull(neq))/1000,1)` thousand households at the national level). A total of `r round(sm(processed$w_sum)/mio,1)` Mt of product were used for processing (including milk). Of these, the largest amount was used to transform maize into flour, primarily for home consumption. All crops entering processing were fully utilised across main and by-product not leaving any waste.

```{r include=FALSE}
produce_long[, itemcount :=ifelse(sold>0, 1, 0)] # number of items consumed
# all in kg!

sale <- produce_long[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by=.(y4_hhid, category, product_type, type, subtype)]

# replace weights
sales <- sale %>% 
  select(!weight_adj) %>% 
  weigh()

sales_stats <- sales %>% 
  filter(produced>0) %>% # many empties, some of them not clear why
  filter(product_type!="Processed crop" & product_type!="other") %>% # check milk, eggs filter  
  mutate(
    perc_sold = sold*100/produced # that's for each category
  ) %>% 
  group_by(category, product_type, type, subtype) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted sample
    n_obs = length(sold[sold>0]), # number of observed sellers
    perc = n_obs/nrow(ag), # percentage of sellers among agricultural households (more meaningful if among producers)
    percprod = n_obs/length(produced[produced>0]), # percentage of producers that sell
    average = mean(sold[sold>0]), # mean quantities sold of producers
    pav = mean(sold*100/produced), # mean proportion sold from production (across sample, makes it comparable to weighted)
    pavsales = mean(sold[sold>0]*100/produced[sold>0]), # mean proportion of production sold by sellers...
    pavsales2 = mn(perc_sold[sold[sold>0]]), # should be the same as pavsales..
    # weighted sample
    neq = sum(weight_adj[sold>0]), # equivalent of sellers
    pneq = neq*100/ag_eq, # percentage sellers of all households included
    neqprod = sum(weight_adj[produced>0]), # producers
    pneqprod = neq*100/sum(weight_adj[produced>0]), # percentage of sellers among producers
    sales = sum(w_sold), # total sales
    av_w = sales/neq, # average sale per household... mmmh not fully sure this is accurate....
    psale = sum(w_sold)*100/sum(w_produced)) %>%  # proportion of total production sold
  filter(n_obs>0) %>% 
  rd()

sales_stats
```

Total sales volume amounted to `r round(sm(sales_stats$sales)/mio,1)` Mt. Of all food groups, sample households were most likely to sell milk (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Milk") %>% pull(pneqprod)`% of food producing households), followed by spices, cash crops and nuts & seeds (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Spices") %>% pull(pneqprod)`, `r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Cashcrops") %>% pull(pneqprod)` and `r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Nuts & seeds") %>% pull(pneqprod)`% of food producing households, respectively). In terms of proportion of production of food crops sold, the highest proportion are sold for vegetables (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Vegetables") %>% pull(psale)`%, an average of `r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Vegetables") %>% pull(av_w)` kg annually per selling household), spices (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Spices") %>% pull(psale)`%) and nuts & seeds (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Nuts & seeds") %>% pull(psale)`%). In absolute terms the largest food group for sales were grains & cereals (`r round(sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Grains & cereals") %>% pull(sales) %>% sc_mt(),1)` Mt), with an annual average of (`r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Grains & cereals") %>% pull(av_w)` kg per selling household), followed by fruits (`r round(sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Fruits") %>% pull(sales) %>% sc_mt(),1)` Mt, or `r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Fruits") %>% pull(av_w)` kg per selling household)) and milk (`r round(sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Milk") %>% pull(sales) %>% sc_mt(),1)` Mt, or `r sales_stats %>% filter(subtype == "Milk") %>% pull(av_w)` kg per selling household). Figure \@ref(fig:b1fig) shows the proportion of sales transactions by location and buyer. More than half of all transactions are conducted in village proximity, with an even allocation to formal and informal markets.

```{r sales-tab, echo=FALSE}
# consistency in naming etc. should happen on top/simplify for all chunks using mfadata
# transf <- mfadata$crops
# transf <- transf[,.(y4_hhid, cropid, type, harvest, sold, payment, gifts, transfer)]
# transf <- transf[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by= .(y4_hhid, type)]
# transf <- weights(transf)
# 
# # range of transfers
# transtab <- transf %>%
#   filter(!is.na(type)) %>% 
#   group_by(type) %>%
#   summarise(
#     gif = sum(gifts),
#     neqgif = sum(weight_adj[gifts>0]), # hh eq gifting
#     # gifp = sum(weight_adj[gifts>0])*100/ag_eq,
#     gifp = neqgif*100/sum(weight_adj[harvest>0]),
#     pay = sum(payment),
#     neqpay = sum(weight_adj[payment>0]), # hh eq payment
#     # payp = sum(weight_adj[payment>0])*100/ag_eq),
#     payp = neqpay*100/sum(weight_adj[harvest>0])
#     )%>%
#   mutate(across(c("gif", "pay"), sct)) %>% 
#   mutate(neqgif = neqgif/ht,
#          neqpay = neqpay/ht,
#          type = str_to_sentence(type)) %>% 
#   rd()
```

```{r b1fig, fig.cap="Proportion of sales by location and buyer type", fig.align='center', out.width="90%", echo=FALSE}
 b1 <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "b1.csv")) %>% 
  weigh() %>% 
  mutate(w_sold = sold*weight_adj) %>% 
  dplyr::rename(type = type_new) # where does _new come from??
# table(b1$new_name)
# table(b1$new_location)

# Allocation as alluvial by frequency - x
summary_data <- b1 %>%
  # filter(product != "cashcrops") %>%
#  group_by(type, new_name, new_location) %>%
  group_by(new_name, new_location) %>%
  summarise(
    TotalSales = n(), .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>% 
  mutate(Proportion = TotalSales / sum(TotalSales)) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(new_name)) %>% 
  rename(BuyerType = new_name, Location = new_location)

# Visualize the data using a heatmap
ggplot(summary_data, aes(x = Location, y = BuyerType, fill = Proportion)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", size = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low ="lightblue", high = "darkblue", labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title = ,
    x = "Location",
    y = "Buyer type",
    fill = "Proportion of total sales"
  ) +
  theme_thesis(colour_type = NULL) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, size = 1)  # Add a black border
  )
```

```{r include=FALSE}
produce_long[, itemcount :=ifelse(consumed>0, 1, 0)] # number of items consumed

cons <- produce_long[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by=.(y4_hhid, category, product_type, type, subtype)]

# replace weights
consumption <- cons %>% 
  select(!weight_adj) %>% 
  weigh()

cons_stat <- consumption %>% 
  filter(produced>0) %>% # many empties, some of them not clear why
  filter(product_type!="Processed crop" & product_type!="other") %>% # check milk, eggs filter  
  group_by(category, product_type, type, subtype) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted sample
    n_obs = length(consumed[consumed>0]), # households consuming
    perc = n_obs*100/nrow(ag), # consumed fo all households.
    average = mean(consumed), # mean consumption of producers
    pav = mean(consumed*100/produced), # mean proportion consumed from production (across sample, makes it comparable to weighted)
    pavconsumers = mean(consumed[consumed>0]*100/produced[consumed>0]), # mean proportion of production consumed by consuming household
    # weighted sample
    neq = sum(weight_adj[consumed>0]),
    pneq = neq*100/ag_eq, # percentage consumers of all households included
    consumption = sum(w_consumed), # total consumption
    av_w = consumption/neq, # average consumption of consumers
    pcons = sum(w_consumed)/sum(w_produced), # proportion of total production consumed
    avitems = mean(itemcount[consumed>0])) %>%  # item count for consumers only
  arrange(desc(perc)) %>% 
  rd() %>% 
  ungroup()

cons_stat
```

```{r end-tab, echo=FALSE}
# 🚩 UNHEALTHY: repeated block — candidate for loop or purrr::map()
produce_long <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "produce_long.csv")) %>% setDT()

# Reduce households to food group
produce_long[, itemcount :=ifelse(produced>0, 1, 0)] # number of items produced

sc1 <- produce_long[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by= .(y4_hhid, category, product_type, type)] # type is smallest category - milk, eggs should be types at next run!

# slaughter product is only inedible stuff, while animal product contains all the meat/offal for the first summary table this needs to be adjusted (may be the source of the error of totals??)

# replace weights
tab4 <- sc1 %>% 
  select(!weight_adj) %>% 
  weigh()

tab4gr <- tab4 %>%
  filter(grepl("crop", product_type)) %>% 
  mutate(perc_cons = consumed*100/produced,
             perc_sold = sold*100/produced) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(type) %>%
  summarise(
    neqprod = sum(weight_adj[produced>0]),
    consumed_tot = sum(consumed),
    perc_cons = sum(consumed)*100/sum(produced),
    w_consumed = sm(w_consumed),
    mn_consumed = mn(consumed),
    neqc = sum(weight_adj[consumed>0]),
    # neqcp = neqc*100/neqprod, # move to more detailed section
    neqcp = neqc*100/ag_eq, # Alternative: percentage of all ag households
    hh_obsc = length(consumed[consumed>0]),
    sold_tot = sum(sold),
    perc_sold = sold_tot/sum(produced)*100,
    neqs = sum(weight_adj[sold>0]),
    # neqsp = neqs*100/neqprod, # move to more detailed section
    neqsp = neqs*100/ag_eq, # Alternative: percentage of all ag households
    hh_obss = length(sold[sold>0])
  ) %>% 
  rd()
```

In terms of consumption more than half of all roots & tubers produced were allocated to household consumption (`r tab4gr %>% filter(grepl("tubers", type)) %>% pull(perc_cons)`% of total production volume), followed by grains & cereal (`r tab4gr %>% filter(grepl("cereals", type)) %>% pull(perc_cons)`%), fruits (`r tab4gr %>% filter(grepl("Fruits", type)) %>% pull(perc_cons)`%), and legumes (`r tab4gr %>% filter(grepl("Legumes", type)) %>% pull(perc_cons)`%). The smallest proportion of production allocated to household consumption were vegetables (`r tab4gr %>% filter(grepl("Vege", type)) %>% pull(perc_cons)`%). Detailed food consumption flows are subject of Chapter \@ref(chapter4).

### Waste and unallocated flows

```{r phl text new, include=FALSE}
phlosses <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "phlosses.csv")) %>% setDT()

# some duplication
phlosses <- phlosses[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by=.(y4_hhid, cropid, type)]
phlosses <- phlosses %>% 
  mutate(across(c(produced:losses), ~.* weight_adj, .names = "w_{col}"),
         n_lost = ifelse(losses>0, 1, 0),
         n_crops = ifelse(produced>0,1,0))

# Quantity, proportion and count lost for each crop, including weighting - x
top10 <- phlosses[, `:=`(
  perc = losses*100/produced)]
# top 10 lost --> to compare with APHLIS once weighted; all much lower...
top10 <- top10 %>%
  filter(produced>0) %>% 
  # mutate(w_perc = w_losses*100/w_produced) %>% 
  group_by(type, cropid) %>%
  summarise(
    # unweighted
    n_obs = n(), # number of households growing crop
    n = sum(n_lost), # number of times the crop was lost/same as number of households affected; weighted eq to weight_adj as hh
    lost = sum(losses),
    perc_mn = mean(perc[losses>0]), # average losses of losers
    perc = sum(losses)*100/sum(produced[losses>0]),
    perctot = sum(losses)*100/sum(produced),
    # weighted
    w_n = sum(weight_adj[n_lost>0]), # hh equivalents affected/times the crop was lost 
    w_lost = sum(w_losses),
    w_perc = sum(w_losses)*100/sum(w_produced[w_losses>0]), # percentage lost among losers
    w_perctot = sum(w_losses)*100/sum(w_produced), # percentage lost among producers
    ) %>% 
  rd()
  
top10 %>% 
  arrange(desc(perc)) %>% 
  filter(lost>0 & n>1)
```

```{r phl figure, include=FALSE}
phtype <- phlosses[, lapply(.SD, sm), .SDcols=is.numeric, by=.(y4_hhid, type)]

phl <- phtype %>% 
  mutate(p_lost = losses*100/produced) %>% 
  filter(type != "cashcrops") %>% 
  select(!weight_adj) %>% 
  weigh() %>% 
  # filter(losses>0) %>% 
  group_by(type) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted
    n_obs = n(),
    lost = sm(losses),
    unw_p_losers = sm(losses)*100/sm(produced[losses>0]),
    mn_p = mn(p_lost[losses>0]),
    unw_p = lost*100/sum(produced),
    # weighted
    w_lost = sum(w_losses),
    perc = w_lost*100/sum(w_produced), # all lost relative to all produced
    neq = sum(weight_adj[w_losses>0]), # hh eq with losses
    neq_p = neq*100/sum(weight_adj[w_produced>0]), # proportion of hh eq producing with losses
    avprod = sum(w_produced[w_losses>0])/neq, # av production of hh eq affected 
    av = w_lost/neq, # average losses per hh eq affected
    # pm = mean(perc),
    pmn = av*100/avprod # av perc loss per hh eq
    ) %>% 
  arrange(desc(w_lost)) %>% 
  filter(lost>0) %>% 
  rd()

processing <- mfa_unweighted <- fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "mfa_unweighted.csv")) %>% filter(grepl("processing", source) | grepl("processing", target) | grepl("waste", target))
processed_crops <- (48588.5+4359.5+18+525324.7)
```

Across all production a total of `r round(sum(phl$w_lost)/mio,1)` Mt (`r sm(phl$perc)`%) of total food crop production were estimated to have been lost at the post-harvest stage. Overall, the largest quantity of post-harvest losses occurred for grains & cereals, yet this represented less than 1% of their total production volume. Relative post-harvest losses were highest for vegetables. Producers of fruits, grains & cereals and vegetables were most commonly affected by post-harvest losses (`r phl %>% filter(type == "fruits") %>%  pull(neq_p)`, `r phl %>% filter(type == "grains & cereals") %>%  pull(neq_p)` and `r phl %>% filter(type == "vegetables") %>%  pull(neq_p)`% of producers, respectively). Among affected households, average total losses were highest for vegetables (`r phl %>% filter(type == "vegetables") %>%  pull(av)` kg), roots & tubers (`r phl %>% filter(type == "roots & tubers") %>%  pull(av)` kg) and grains & cereals (`r phl %>% filter(type == "grains & cereals") %>%  pull(av)` kg). Relative to total production volume of affected households highest average losses were recorded for roots & tubers (`r phl %>% filter(type == "roots & tubers") %>%  pull(pmn)`%), vegetables <!--(`r phl %>% filter(type == "vegetables") %>%  pull(pmn)`%)--> and fruits <!--(`r phl %>% filter(type == "fruits") %>%  pull(pmn)`%)-->, both 19.1%. All food groups had at least one food item of which a substantial (\>25%) volume lost post-harvest. <!--Add table? (table \ref(tab:)) *More about individual %>% of interest*--> On average, when lost, the highest average proportion is lost by cassava growers (`r top10 %>% filter(cropid == "cassava") %>% pull(w_perc)`%), followed by mango (`r top10 %>% filter(cropid == "mango") %>% pull(w_perc)`), tomato (`r top10 %>% filter(cropid == "tomatoes") %>% pull(w_perc)`%), and pigeon pea (`r top10 %>% filter(cropid == "pigeon pea") %>% pull(w_perc)`%). In absolute terms maize and mangos are lost most, `r round(top10 %>% filter(cropid == "maize") %>% pull(w_lost)/mio,1)` Mt or `r top10 %>% filter(cropid == "mango") %>% pull(w_perc)`% of total production. In addition to postharvest losses, crops also generated processing waste which was estimated to have been `r round(29639.7*100/processed_crops,1)`% of the total volume used for processing.

````{=tex}
\newpage
\begin{landscape}

```{r phl, echo=FALSE}
phl %>% 
  mutate(type = str_to_sentence(type),
         lost = lost/kt,
         w_lost = w_lost/mio,
         neq = neq/kt
         ) %>% 
  rd() %>% 
  select(type,
         n_obs, lost, unw_p,
         neq, neq_p, w_lost, perc, av, pmn) %>% 

  kbltbl(
    caption = "Post-harvest losses",
    col_names = c("Group", 
                  "Producers affected", "Volume lost (t)", "Average losses (%)",
                  "1000s", "%", # Household equivalents
                  "kt", "%", # total losses
                  "kg", "%" # average losses
                  )) %>% 
    add_header_above(c(
      " " = 4,
      "Household equivalents" = 2,
      "Total losses" = 2,
      "Average losses" = 2
    )) %>%
    add_header_above(c(
      " "= 1,
      "Unweigted sample" = 3,
      "Weighted sample" = 6
    )) %>% 
  column_spec(1, width = "2cm") %>%
  column_spec(2:10, width = "1.5cm") 
  
```

\end{landscape}
\newpage
````

```{r, echo=FALSE}
sl_waste <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "sl_waste.csv"))
```

Based on the number of animals slaughtered for consumption the estimation of truly inedible parts (all parts beside meat and offal) of slaughtered animals amounted to `r round(sl_waste %>% filter(type == "poultry") %>% pull(w_totined)/mio,1)` Mt for poultry alone, followed by `r round(sl_waste %>% filter(type == "small ruminants") %>% pull(w_totined)/mio,1)` Mt and `r round(sl_waste %>% filter(type == "large ruminants") %>% pull(w_totined)/mio,1)` Mt for small and large ruminants. Of the total weight of skins and hides available from slaughtered animals, a third was used to be further processed, with the remainder presumably to have been discarded.

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Run in order to update analysis (after any changes to cleaning process/inclusion)
# source("03-MCS_household.R")
# source("03_MFA.R")
# source("03-sankey.R")
# source("03-uncertainty.R")
# source("03-performance.R")
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
# summary table, weighted, unweighted of missing by food group 
# dataframe written below
# positive <- observed_wide %>% filter(perc_miss>0)

missingtab <- observed_wide_fin %>% 
  group_by(group, type) %>% 
  summarise(
    # unweighted data
    n = n(),
    n_pos = length(positive[positive>0]), # "waste" households
    n_neg = length(negative[negative>0]), # "over-users"
    sumpos = sm(positive)/kt,
    meanpos = mn(positive[produced>0]), # mean across producers only
    sdpos = sd(positive[produced>0]),
    meanppos = mn(p_positive[produced>0]),
    sumneg = sm(negative)/kt, # total uncertain from overestimate
    # weighted data
    neq = sum(weight_adj[produced>0])/kt, # number of household equivalents producing
    neq_pos = sum(weight_adj[positive>0])/kt, # household equivalent with losses
    neq_neg = sum(weight_adj[negative>0])/kt, # household equivalent with losses
    sumwpos = sm(w_positive)/mio,
    # ptot = sm(w_positive)*100/sm(w_produced),
    meanpposw = sm(w_positive)*100/sm(w_produced),
    meanwpos = sm(w_positive)/(neq*kt),
    sumwneg = sm(w_negative)/mio, # total uncertain from overestimate
  ) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  rd()
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
posonly <- observed_wide_fin %>%
  filter(dest_missing>0) %>%
  group_by(group, type) %>%
  summarise(
    # unweighted data
    n = n(),
    # n_pos = length(positive[positive>0]), # "waste" households
    # n_neg = length(negative[negative>0]), # "over-users"
    sumpos = sm(positive)/kt,
    meanpos = mn(positive[produced>0]), # mean across producers only
    sdpos = sd(positive[produced>0]),
    meanppos = mn(p_positive[produced>0]),
#    sumneg = sm(negative)/kt, # total uncertain from overestimate
    # weighted data
    neq = sum(weight_adj[produced>0])/kt, # number of household equivalents producing
    # neq_pos = sum(weight_adj[positive>0])/kt, # household equivalent with losses
    # neq_neg = sum(weight_adj[negative>0])/kt, # household equivalent with losses
    sumwpos = sm(w_positive)/mio,
    meanwpos = sm(w_positive)/(neq*kt),
    meanpposw = sm(w_positive)*100/sm(w_produced),
#    sumwneg = sm(w_negative)/t/t, # total uncertain from overestimate
  ) %>%
  rd()
```

<!-- Unallocated flows across food groups and households, breakdown table -->

In addition to clearly lost volumes, some of production volume remained unallocated/missing. On average `r round(mn(missingtab$meanpposw),1)`% of households production was not allocated to a destination (`r round(sum(observed_wide_fin$w_positive)/totalneq,1)` kg per household across food groups) (Table \@ref(tab:missingtab)). The largest unaccounted volume was determined for grains & cereals (`r max(missingtab$sumwpos)`kt), representing `r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("grains", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`% of total production. The food group with the largest proportion of missing volumes relative to production was milk (`r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`%). The highest average missing volume per household was for milk (`r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("milk", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)` kg), followed by fruits (`r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("fruits", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)` kg) and grains (`r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("grains", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)` kg). Only considering the households that had a positive mass balance, average missing flows were as high as `r posonly %>% filter(grepl("fruit", type)) %>% pull(meanwpos)` kg for fruit (`r posonly %>% filter(grepl("fruit", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`%), `r posonly %>% filter(grepl("milk", type)) %>% pull(meanwpos)` kg for milk (`r posonly %>% filter(grepl("milk", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`% of production), and `r posonly %>% filter(grepl("grains", type)) %>% pull(meanwpos)` kg (`r posonly %>% filter(grepl("grains", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`%) for grains and cereals. The total volume of eggs unallocated remained at `r round(missingtab %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(sumwpos),1)` kg or `r missingtab %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`% across the entire sample and `r posonly %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(meanpposw)`% (`r posonly %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(meanwpos)`kg) across households with a positive mass balance.

````{=tex}
\newpage
\begin{landscape}

```{r missingtab, echo=FALSE}
missingtab %>% 
  arrange(desc(group)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  select(!group) %>% 
  filter(!grepl("slaughter", type)) %>%
  mutate(type = str_to_sentence(type)) %>% 
  kbltbl(
    caption = "Overview of positive mass discrepancies by food group",
    col_names = c(" ", 
                  "Producers", "Positive balance", "Negative balance",
                  "Total (t)", "Average (kg)", "SD", "%",
                  "Total negative (t)",
                  "Producers", "Positive balance", "Negative balance",
                  "Total (t)", "%", "Average (kg)", 
                  "Total negative (kt)"
                  ),
    longtable = T) %>% 
    add_header_above(c(
      " " = 1,
      "Number of households" = 3,
      "Missing volume" = 4, 
      " " = 1,
      "Household equivalents (1000s)" = 3,
      "Missing volume" = 3,
      " " = 1)) %>% 
    add_header_above(c(
      " " = 1,
      "Unweigted sample" = 8,
      "Weighted sample" = 7
    )) %>%
  pack_rows(
    index = c("Crops" = 9, "Animal products" = 3)) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "2cm") %>%
  column_spec(2:16, width = "1cm") 
```
\end{landscape}
\newpage
````

### Data uncertainty

```{r echo=FALSE}
# hosuehold food group
hhrangegroup <- observed_wide_fin %>% 
  filter(produced>0) 

# group range
grouprange <- hhrangegroup %>% 
  group_by(type) %>% 
  summarise(
    produced = sum(produced),
    negative = sum(negative),
    positive = sum(positive)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    diff = positive-negative,
    pdiff = diff*100/produced) %>%   # net
  rd()

# household balance
hhrange <- hhrangegroup %>% 
  group_by(y4_hhid) %>% 
  summarise(
    produced = sum(produced),
    negative = sum(negative),
    positive = sum(positive)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    diff = positive-negative,
    pdiff = diff/produced) %>%   # net
  rd()

# total balance
totalbalance <- observed_wide_fin %>% 
  group_by(y4_hhid) %>% 
  summarise(
    produced = sum(produced),
    negative = sum(negative),
    positive = sum(positive)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    meanpos = mean(positive),
    meanneg = mean(negative),
    sumpos = sum(positive),
    sumneg = sum(negative),
    sumproduced = sum(produced)
  ) %>% 
  rd()
```

Following the principles of conservation of mass, the negative mass balance for any given household was set to zero and the difference was reallocated across flows. Notwithstanding, we assumed that negative as well as positive net balance (as reported above) are at least partially due to measurement error. Overall, the mass balance of `r round(hhrange %>% filter(diff==0) %>% nrow()*100/nrow(hhrange),1)`% households across food groups reported was balanced and `r round(hhrange %>% filter(diff<0) %>% nrow()*100/nrow(hhrange),1)`% negative (compared to `r round(hhrange %>% filter(diff>0) %>% nrow()*100/nrow(hhrange),1)`% positive). Across all households the negative balance accumulated to an average of `r totalbalance %>% pull(meanneg)` kg of the total production volume, compared to `r totalbalance %>% pull(meanpos)` kg of produce in positive balance. The distribution of negative and positive mass balances across households and food groups is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:distr)).

```{r distr, fig.cap="Distribution of negative vs. positive mass balance by food group", fig.align='center', out.width="90%", echo=FALSE}
hhrangegroup %>%
  mutate(
    p_severity = p_positive - p_negative, # optional, requires more explanation for animal products, Appendix?
    total_severity = positive - negative,
    # w_total_severity = w_positive/weight_adj - w_negative/weight_adj, # the same no?
    perc = total_severity / produced,
    type = str_to_sentence(type)
  ) %>% 
  rename(value = total_severity) %>%
  filter(!grepl("slaughter", type)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = type, y = value, color = value > 0)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = type, xend = type, y = 0, yend = value), size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "#1F77B4", "FALSE" = "#FF7F0E")  # Tableau 10 Blue and Orange
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Food group",
    y = "Total discrepancy (kg)",
    caption = "Slaughtered animals not displayed."
  ) +
  theme_thesis(colour_type = NULL) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

The largest proportion of negative mass balance was recorded for vegetables (`r grouprange %>% filter(grepl("vegetables", type)) %>% pull(pdiff)*-1`% relative to total production was over-allocated). For most other food groups (except 'other' and cashcrops) the final balance across households was positive. The largest positive difference was estimated for eggs (`r grouprange %>% filter(grepl("eggs", type)) %>% pull(pdiff)`%), followed by milk (`r grouprange %>% filter(grepl("milk - large", type)) %>% pull(pdiff)`%) and legumes (`r grouprange %>% filter(grepl("legumes", type)) %>% pull(pdiff)`%). A comparison of overall negative and positive mass balance across food groups is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:negpos).

```{r negpos, fig.cap="Comparison of negative vs. positive discrepancy by food group", fig.align='center', out.width="90%", echo=FALSE}
# Summarize the data
summary_data <- hhrangegroup %>% 
  group_by(type) %>% 
  summarise(
    totneg = sum(negative, na.rm = TRUE),  # Include na.rm = TRUE for robustness
    totpos = sum(positive, na.rm = TRUE),
    totprod = sum(produced, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(
    total_severity = totpos - totneg,  # Calculate total severity
    proportion = ifelse(totprod == 0, NA, total_severity / totprod),  # Avoid division by zero
    type = str_to_sentence(type)  # Ensure type is in sentence case
  ) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(type) & !grepl("Slaughter", type))  # Exclude "slaughter" types and handle NA

# Visualization: Compare Negative vs. Positive Severity
summary_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = type, y = total_severity, fill = total_severity > 0)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "#1F77B4", "FALSE" = "#FF7F0E"),  # Tableau 10 Blue and Orange
    labels = c("Positive", "Negative")
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Food group",
    y = "Total discrepancy (kg)",
    fill = "Sign",  # Legend title
    caption = "Results for unweighted data."
  ) +
  theme_thesis(colour_type = NULL) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, size = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# uncertainty across households
neg_observed <- observed_data %>% 
  anti_join(excl_3a) %>% 
  filter(observed_value<0) %>% select(y4_hhid) %>% unique() %>% left_join(weighting) 

wide_neg <- observed_wide_fin
wide_neg %>%
  group_by(range_group) %>%
  summarise(count = n(),
            sum = sm(dest_missing),
            meanp = mean(perc_miss[perc_miss>0]),
            meanpn = mean(perc_miss[perc_miss<0])*-1)
            

neg_group <- wide_neg %>%
  group_by(type, range_group) %>%
  summarise(count = n(),
            sum = sm(dest_missing),
            meanp = mn(perc_miss))
            
negative_data <- neg_group %>%
  filter(range_group == "Negative") %>%
  group_by(type) %>%
  summarise(
    total_negative_count = sum(count),
    total_negative_severity = sum(sum),
    average_negative_severity = mean(meanp))


# uncertainty after propagating negative values
household_mcs <- fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "household_mcs.csv"))
```

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Filter for Negative and Positive rows
filtered_data <- neg_group %>%
  filter(range_group %in% c("Negative", "Postive")) %>%
  group_by(type, range_group) %>%
  summarise(
    total_count = sum(count),
    total_severity = sum(sum),
    average_severity = mean(meanp)
  )

# Calculate proportion of "Negative" rows
overall_data <- neg_group %>%
  group_by(type) %>%
  summarise(total_count = sum(count))

summary_data <- negative_data %>%
  left_join(overall_data, by = "type") %>%
  mutate(proportion_negative = total_negative_count / total_count)




# Calculate the overall count for each type
overall_data <- neg_group %>%
  group_by(type) %>%
  summarise(total_count_all = sum(count))
```

```{r eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE}
# Wide format transformation and quintile calculation
# negative households
negative_households <- purrr::map_dfr(
  c("negative_meat", "negative_crops", "negative_ap"),
  ~ fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", paste0(.x, ".csv"))) |>
      group_by(type, y4_hhid = household_id, destination) |>
      summarise(mean = mean(simulated_values), sd = sd(simulated_values), .groups = "drop")
)
negsim <- negative_households %>% 
  setDT() %>% 
  dcast(
  y4_hhid + type ~ destination, value.var = "mean"
) 
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
household_mcs <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "household_mcs.csv")) %>% setDT()
mcs_weighted <- fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "mcs_weighted.csv")) %>% setDT()
system_uncertainty <- household_mcs[, .(
  Sum = sum(mean_value),                          # Sum of values
  SD_Sum = sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),                  # Combined SD of the sum
  CI_Lower = sum(mean_value) - 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)), # Lower bound of 95% CI
  CI_Upper = sum(mean_value) + 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),
  Percentage_Uncertainty = (sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)) / sum(mean_value)) * 100# Upper bound of 95% CI
)]
```

````{=tex}
\newpage
\begin{landscape}
```{r uncertainty, echo=FALSE}
left <- household_mcs[, .(
  Sum = sum(mean_value),                          # Sum of values
  SD_Sum = sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),                  # Combined SD of the sum
  CI_Lower = sum(mean_value) - 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)), # Lower bound of 95% CI
  CI_Upper = sum(mean_value) + 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),
  Percentage_Uncertainty = (sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)) / sum(mean_value)) * 100# Upper bound of 95% CI
), by = c("type", "destination")]

right <- mcs_weighted[, .(
  Sum = sum(mean_value),                          # Sum of values
  SD_Sum = sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),                  # Combined SD of the sum
  CI_Lower = sum(mean_value) - 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)), # Lower bound of 95% CI
  CI_Upper = sum(mean_value) + 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),
  Percentage_Uncertainty = (sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)) / sum(mean_value)) * 100# Upper bound of 95% CI
), by = c("type", "destination")]

uncertainty <- left %>% 
  full_join(right, by = c("type", "destination")) %>%
  rd() %>% 
  filter(grepl("missing|waste|loss|inedible", destination)) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(Percentage_Uncertainty.x)) %>% 
  filter(Sum.x>0) %>%  # no waste for slaughtered animals (contained in inedible)
  # mutate(
  #   across(3:6, ~./kt)
  # ) %>% 
  mutate(
    across(8:11, ~./mio)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(type = str_to_sentence(type))

uncertainty %>% 
  rd() %>% 
  arrange(desc(destination)) %>%
  select(-destination) %>% 
  kbltbl(
    caption = "Uncertainty of inefficiency flows by food group",
    col_names = c("Food group", 
                  "Sum (kg)", "SD (kg)", "Lower bound (kg)", "Upper bound (kg)",
                  "% uncertainty",
                  "Sum (Mt)", "SD (Mt)", "Lower bound (Mt)", "Upper bound (Mt)",
                  "% uncertainty")
  ) %>% 
  add_header_above(c(
    " " = 1,
    "Unweighted sample" = 5,
    "Weighted sample" = 5
  )) %>%
  pack_rows(
     index = c("Missing flow" = 10, "Losses flow" = 8, "Slaughter waste flow" = 4)
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "3cm") %>%
  column_spec(2:11, width = "1.5cm") 

```
\end{landscape}
\newpage
````

Overall system-level uncertainty after applying the MCS approach showed a percentage uncertainty of 0.3% for the unweighted and 0.4% for the weighted data. Table \@ref(tab:uncertainty) shows the uncertainty of the inefficiency flows across food groups. Finally, the goodness of fit metrics when comparing the observed with the simulated data showed a moderate fit of the model with 62.6% of the variance explained and a Mean Absolute Error of 59.8.

```{r echo=FALSE}
# 🚩 UNHEALTHY: repeated block — candidate for loop or purrr::map()
mcs_weighted <- fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "mcs_weighted.csv")) %>% setDT()

group_uncertainty_weighted <- mcs_weighted[, .(
  Sum = sum(mean_value),                          # Sum of values
  SD_Sum = sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),                  # Combined SD of the sum
  CI_Lower = sum(mean_value) - 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)), # Lower bound of 95% CI
  CI_Upper = sum(mean_value) + 1.96 * sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)),
  Percentage_Uncertainty = (sqrt(sum(sd_value^2)) / sum(mean_value)) * 100  # Upper bound of 95% CI
), by = type]
```

### Visualisation of material flows

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Breakdown of single categories, useful for inline code
# revise all below
summary_crops <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "summary_crops.csv")) %>% 
  select(type, produced, upper, lower, mean, sd) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(type)) %>% 
  mutate(lower = as.numeric(lower))
mfaw <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "mfaw.csv"))
household <- mfaw %>% filter(target == "Household")
sales <- mfaw %>% filter(target == "Sales")
flw <- mfaw %>% filter(grepl("Loss", target))
unallocated <- mfaw %>% filter(target == "Unallocated")
gifts <- mfaw %>% filter(target == "Gifts" | target == "Payment")
total <- sec1 %>% filter(!(grepl("Processed", product_type)))
total <- sm(total$w_produced)

missing <- mfaw %>% filter(target == "Missing")

lvstcktot <- animals_fin %>% group_by(lvstckid) %>% 
  summarise(produced = sm(w_slaughter))

smtotal <- summary_crops %>% 
  select(type, produced) %>% 
  bind_rows(select(milk_stats, type = subtype, produced = total_prod)) %>% 
  bind_rows(select(lvstcktot, type = lvstckid, produced))

milk <- smtotal %>% filter(type == "Milk")
mtotal <- sm(summary_crops$produced) + sm(milk_stats$total_prod) + sm(animals_fin$w_slaughter) # slightly lower than total
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
list <- readRDS(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "weighted_flows.RDS"))
mfaW <- mfafun(list)

# for appendix
data <- mfaW %>% setDT()
nodes <- data.frame(name = unique(c(as.character(data$source), as.character(data$target))))

# Map source and target to node IDs
data <- data %>%
     mutate(
         IDsource = match(source, nodes$name) - 1,
         IDtarget = match(target, nodes$name) - 1
     )

# Ensure proper capitalization for node names
nodes$name <- stringr::str_to_sentence(nodes$name)

overview_table <- data %>%
     group_by(source, target) %>%
     summarise(total_value = sum(value), .groups = "drop") %>%
     arrange(desc(total_value)) # Sort by descending flow value

# Display the overview table, needs variability
# overview_table %>%
#      kable(
#          caption = "Overview of MFA Data for Sankey Diagram",
#          col.names = c("Source", "Target", "Total Flow Value"),
#          booktabs = TRUE,
#          longtable = TRUE
#      ) %>%
#   column_spec(1:3, width = "3cm") %>%
#   kbltbl()
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
weighted_flows_wide <- fread(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "weighted_flows_wide.csv")) %>% 
  mutate(totsold = ifelse(!is.na(prodsold), prodsold+sold, sold))

totalprod_w <- sm(weighted_flows_wide$produced)

flw <- overview_table %>% filter(grepl("waste", target)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value)) + overview_table %>% filter(grepl("losses", target)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))
```

The Sankey diagram in Figure \@ref(fig:sankey) graphically summarises the flows for all commodities based on the MCS results and extrapolated to national representativeness. The figure highlights the relative contribution of all flows within the system, including loss and waste flows and those assumed to be system inefficiencies (*i.e.*, missing). Examples of individual household Sankey diagrams that have been aggregated to create the nationally representative material flows are shown in subsequent chapters (Chapter \@ref(chapter4) and Chapter \@ref(chapter5)).

The figure shows an almost even split between the allocation of food to household and towards sales, `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "sold") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% and `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "consumed") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of total production, respectively. A further `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "transfer") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of total production was allocated towards the informal food economy as gifts and payments. The Sankey diagram shows visually the relative contribution of each food group to the smallholder production system. Accordingly, grains & cereal represented the largest production entity `r round(overview_table %>% filter(grepl("grains", source)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of the total production, followed by fruits and roots & tubers (`r round(overview_table %>% filter(grepl("fruits", source)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)` and `r round(overview_table %>% filter(grepl("tubers", source)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`%). Milk had the largest production volume of animal products, `r round(overview_table %>% filter(grepl("milk", source)) %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of total production. The total flow of losses and waste represents `r round(flw*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of total production value compared to `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "missing") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))*100/totalprod_w,1)`% of unallocated flows. This is equivalent `r round(flw/mio,1)` Mt of FLW across post-harvest losses, processing waste and inedible produce from slaughter. Unallocated flows amounted to `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "missing") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))/mio,1)` Mt.

```{r sankeyprep3, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# 🚩 UNHEALTHY: repeated block — candidate for loop or purrr::map()
list <- readRDS(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "weighted_flows.RDS"))
mfaW <- mfafun(list)
sankeyfig <- sankey(mfaW)
# Save the Sankey diagram as a static image using orca

htmlwidgets::saveWidget(sankeyfig, "figure/c3/sankey.html", selfcontained = TRUE)
# Take a screenshot of the HTML files as PNG
webshot::webshot("figure/c3/sankey.html", "figure/c3/sankey.png", vwidth=900, vheight=600)
```

````{=tex}
\newpage
\begin{landscape}

```{r sankey, fig.cap = "Sankey diagram showing flows of all production and allocation volumes", out.width = "100%", echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("figure/c3/sankey.png")
```

\end{landscape}
\newpage
````

## Discussion

### Quantifying food allocation and inefficiencies

Food production estimates in this study are largely comparable with other reports. For example, the current estimate of annual maize production of `r round(sec1_detail %>% filter(item == "Maize") %>% pull(w_produced) %>% sc_mt(),1)` Mt is largely comparable to what is estimated in the official LSMS-ISA report (5 Mt) and only slightly below the FAO estimate (5.9 Mt) [@FAOSTAT]. While the estimate of cattle milk production differed more than twofold compared to official Tanzanian statistics (`r round(observed_wide_fin %>% filter(grepl("milk", type)) %>% summarise(sum = sum(w_produced))/tokt,1)` kt vs. 1,905 Mt [@helgilibrary_milk_2014]), most other estimates are much closer to official reports, such as mango which we estimate at `r round(sec1_detail %>% filter(item == "Mango") %>% pull(w_produced)/mio,1)` Mt compared to 427.6 Mt reported for 2015 (440.4 Mt in 2014) [@FAOSTAT]. In terms of inefficiencies, we were able to highlight `r round(flw/mio,1)` Mt of food lost/wasted, and `r round(overview_table %>% filter(target == "missing") %>% summarise(sum = sm(total_value))/mio,1)` Mt as unallocated which stands in a net balance relative to all negative mass balance that we corrected for. It is reasonable to assume that a non-negligible fraction of this balance represents waste. While not all waste can be considered as a sign of inefficiency, yet it is still relevant to quantify this concept to consider the potential of a given (waste) resource. For example, the `r round(sum(sl_waste$w_totined)/mio,1)` Mt of inedible animal material generated from the `r round(sum(animals_fin$w_slcons)/mio,1)` million animals slaughtered for consumption (not considering those on-sold live) has the potential to contribute to food security and income [@Jayathilakan.2012; @Rolker.2022]. Below we provide a discussion of key findings by food group and point of inefficiency.

**Staple crops: grains & cereals and roots & tubers**

Roots & tubers and grains & cereals have been the primary focus of food loss and waste studies to date [@Stathers.2020; @Kaminski.2014; @Tesfaye.2018]. Staple crops are crucial to meet the population's energy requirements, and their storability is a safeguard throughout the year not only in terms of consumption but also as income strategy [@Kumar.2017]. Not surprisingly, therefore, grains & cereals (maize in particular) are central to the production system in Tanzania, and represent the largest production quantity and the largest number of households that engage in production. The estimate of post-harvest losses for maize is `r round(top10 %>% filter(cropid == "maize") %>% mutate(lost = round(lost/kt,1)) %>% pull(lost),1)` t, representing less than 1% of overall maize production and `r round(top10 %>% filter(cropid == "maize") %>% pull(perc),1)`% of the production of households that incurred losses. This value is far below previously reported estimates, including 728,000 t estimated by the FAO [@FAOSTAT] and 789,762 t reported by APHLIS [@APHLIS]. It is however broadly in line with a previous analysis that showed a 19.7% loss among loss-incurring households included in a preceding LSMS-ISA wave for Tanzania [@Kaminski.2014].

**Fruits & vegetables**

```{r availabil, echo=FALSE}
veg <- tab1 %>% filter(type == "Vegetables")
fruit <- tab1 %>% filter(type == "Fruits")
```

Previous analyses have shown that to achieve adequate nutrient supply from fruits and vegetables reducing food loss and waste would be insufficient in Tanzania [@Mason.2019], as only a small proportion of households produce these riskier commodities [@Fakayode.2012]. Notwithstanding, the results here indicate that fruit and vegetables are attractive and valuable, as evidenced by the frequent choice as gifts (they are also often the subject to theft). Although a wide range of losses has previously been reported for vegetables [@Xue.2017], a relatively small proportion of producers in the current sample align with this conclusion. The analysis further translates to a vegetable and fruit availability of `r round((veg %>% pull(production)/veg %>% pull(neq))/365,1)` kg/day for producing households and `r round((fruit %>% pull(production)/fruit %>% pull(neq))/365,1)` kg per fruit producing household. <!-- add per capita information --> Yet, we show further that a large majority of vegetable production is allocated to sales rather than to the household for consumption. This may be explained by perceived health risks due to pesticide use, which makes producers reluctant to consume vegetables [@Ngowi.2007].

**Animal products**

```{r fao data, include=FALSE}
FAOSTAT_all_losses <- read_csv(here::here("data", "processed", "01", "FAOSTAT_all_losses.csv")) %>% setDT() %>% 
  filter(`Year Code` == 2014 | `Year Code` == 2015)
```

Available estimates on losses and wastage of animal products are scarce [@Affognon.2015]. <!--The FAO Food Balance Sheets aim at presenting a comprehensive picture of food production and availability for most countries. As part of this data compilation the FAO provides estimates of food losses across the value chain up to the retail level (_i.e._, post-harvest losses, processing, transport). The estimates are based on reference values from the literature, available local data sources and other information derived from data and text mining approaches [@foodbal2023]. --> The FAO Food Balance Sheet estimates all milk and milk product losses in 2014 and 2015 at 41 and 40 kt, thus the current estimate of `r round(observed_wide_fin %>% filter(grepl("milk", type)) %>% summarise(miss = sum(w_missing))/tokt,1)` kt of milk is less than half for each 2014 and 2015 [@FAO_FBS]. <!-- The FAO estimates egg losses across the value chain at 9 t for each year of interest, giving reasonably comparable values to the estimate of **r round(milk_stats %>% filter(item == "Eggs") %>% pull(w_unsum)/t/t,1)** thousand t. --> It needs to be noted, however, that it is not always apparent from the LSMS-ISA data whether post-wholesale transport losses are included in the data and therefore these values may not cover the same system boundaries. Lastly, the FAO estimates offal losses and discard at 12 thousand t for both years. From the current data it is not possible to provide a disaggregated estimate of how much of the offal of slaughtered animals has been allocated to consumption or was not suitable for human consumption and required condemnation [@Mellau.2011]. Notwithstanding, the estimate of edible offal for animals allocated to household consumption accounts for `r round(sum(sl_waste$w_totoffal)/mio,1)` thousand tons. <!-- \- eggs - meat: consumption of offal possibly following a socio-economic divide [@Baker.2016]  -->

**Pre-harvest relative to post-harvest losses and wastage**

```{r pre-post, include=FALSE}
# weighted data only
# prepare yield gaps
yg_relative <- yieldgaps %>% 
  mutate(
    prelostdum = ifelse(preharvest_losses == "yes", 1,0),
    otherloss = ifelse(lessharvest == "yes", 1, 0)) %>% 
  group_by(y4_hhid, type, item = cropid) %>% 
  summarise(
    n_plots = n(), # number of plots
    n_lost = sum(prelostdum), # number of plots affected by prelosses
    n_other = sum(otherloss),
    yp = sm(w_yp), # total expected yield potential
    yg = sm(w_yg), # total estimated gap
    harvest = sm(w_harvest) # total estimated harvest
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    p_yield = ifelse(
      yp>0, harvest*100/yp, NA), # harvest as percentage of potential
    p_yg = 100-p_yield) # yield forgone preharvest

crops_relative <- produce_long %>% 
  filter(category == "Plant" & product_type != "Processed crop") %>% 
  filter(w_produced>0) %>% 
  select(y4_hhid, item, type, w_produced, w_uncertain) %>% 
  mutate(type = str_to_lower(type),
         item = str_to_lower(item)) %>% 
  left_join(yg_relative) %>% 
  left_join(select(phlosses, y4_hhid, item = cropid, w_losses)) %>% 
  mutate(
    harvest = harvest*1000, # back to kg
    harvest = ifelse(is.na(harvest), w_produced, harvest), # not available for permanent & fruits
    # otherloss = ifelse(n_other>0, harvest - w_produced, NA),
    # p_other = otherloss*100/harvest, # nonsense
    p_harvested = w_produced*100/harvest, # percentage harvested
    p_remaining = 100-p_harvested, # perc yet to be harvested
    p_losses = w_losses*100/w_produced, # once harvested, not of total harvest
    p_unclear = w_uncertain*100/w_produced
  ) %>% 
  rd()

rel_stats <- crops_relative %>% 
  select(y4_hhid, item, type, starts_with("p_"))

fin_percs <- rel_stats %>% 
  filter(!is.infinite(p_harvested)) %>%  # excludes one Inf
  filter(p_yield>0) %>% 
  group_by(item) %>% 
  summarise(
    across(starts_with("p_"), ~ mean(.x, na.rm=T))
  ) %>% 
  rd()

# same for animals
```

For crops with an available yield potential estimate the quantities unaccounted for pre-harvest by far outweigh any losses incurred during or after harvest. In the survey years the harvest across the seven crops available was on average `r round(mean(fin_percs$p_yg),1)`% lower than potential yield. The yield gap estimates here are higher than the estimates provided by the GYGA; for example for maize, we estimate a yield of `r fin_percs %>% filter(item == "maize") %>% pull(p_yield)`% of the yield potential, whereas the GYGP places the estimate at `r round((1.9+1.7)*100/(6.6+6.8),1)`% across the two survey years. These figures contrasts to the relatively minor losses of `r round(mean(fin_percs$p_losses),1)`% post-harvest.

Overall, post-harvest losses make up a relatively minor factor and account for an average of less than one percent across crops and households (`r round(mn(crops_relative$p_losses),1)`%). Given data limitations a distinction between pre-slaughter losses and condemnation or wastage of animal product after slaughter cannot be made. Yet, of the total possible number of livestock owned across those years `r round(sum(animals_fin$w_all_lost)*100/sum(animals_fin$w_owned),1)`% of animals are estimated to have been lost to disease, injury and theft. This compares to `r round(sum(animals_fin$w_slaughter)*100/sum(animals_fin$w_owned),1)`% of animals slaughtered that were subsequently unutilised. As a whole and based on the Sankey diagram constructed the system seems to indicate relatively low levels of overall wastage. Whether at the household level these can be considered efficient or maximising household benefits in light of potential trade-offs requires further investigation (see Chapter \@ref(chapter6)).

### Reflections on the methodology

An MFA has several advantages at displaying a system resource use. Firstly, it allows a perspective on a range of activities and materials within a system and compares their overall impact across system materials and activities [@Brunner.2016]. The method can be used at any scale from human metabolism to the path of a single item [@Amicarelli.2020b] to global food systems [@Alexander.2017]. In the first instance it is easily conducted and relies on simple mathematical balancing that follows the law of conservation of matter. The use of data from a range of sources to trace and cross-reference material flows and the ability to alter, adjust and improve assumptions when estimating missing allocations (*i.e.*, use of *proxy data* and improved algorithms), improves reliability and allows for flexibility when modelling material flows. As such, the validity of an MFA largely depends on the quality of the underlying data; cross-referencing and reconciling data across multiple sources can result in good estimates of material flows that include a range of uncertainty to represent a system and guide decision making [@Brunner.2016].

**Mass as a measure of outcome**

The first step of any MFA is establishing the mass of material flows before the path and concentration of certain *substances* contained in the materials can be established [@Brunner.2016]. Depending on the research question and commodity types at hand, however, mass as a mid-point outcome measure of inefficiency can only be a crude indicator of the implications of the waste generated. It is reasonably common to estimate food waste in an MFA using mass as a measure of outcome [@Amicarelli.2020b; @Caldeira.2019; @Ju.2017; @Courtonne.2015], in particular when the initial purpose lies in quantifying waste. To highlight the implications of food systems inefficiencies mass estimates can be expressed using other indicators. This can be particularly meaningful when the inefficiencies across multiple food groups and production systems are presented comparatively. Other means of expressing food system inefficiencies to improve compatibility include dry matter, protein and energy [@Alexander.2017], embedded water, crop land, fertiliser [@Kummu.2012] and carbon footprint [@Jiang.2023]. Expressing material flows in terms of their environmental implications in particular has become common and covers a range of contexts (see Chapter \@ref(chapter5)) [@Jiang.2023; @Kovanda.2022; @Amicarelli.2021a].

In this regard, the primary purpose of this chapter was to quantify food system inefficiencies and thus weight or mass is meaningful as it highlights what items and food groups are most affected and where interventions can be targeted. Ultimately, this mass based quantification effort can be seen as a proxy and is further expanded on in subsequent chapters that highlight the wider implications of the material flows in terms of the substances contained within the weight measure.

**Suitability of MFA based on LSMS-ISA data**

In this chapter an MFA using LSMS-ISA survey data was constructed. Overall, the survey provided sufficiently detailed information that was coherent in reporting to construct a MFA. Yet, this may be a unique feature of the Tanzanian dataset as initial (non-holistic) attempts to replicate this analysis with other country datasets (Uganda, Ethiopia, Niger, Nigeria, Malawi) were either unsuccessful or deemed substantially more time intensive due to needs for additional data manipulation. The production, sales, consumption and loss estimates were generally aligned to other reports, although for a subset of commodities the representativeness of the data warrants further investigation. <!-- This chapter builds the basis of subsequent analyses in this thesis. Here, I show inefficiencies and waste up to the point of sale/consumption, however, I are unable to infer that these quantities are used (fully) at the point of destination. The next chapter will estimate the mismatch between reported production for consumption and consumption from production and from there extrapolate overall waste quantities generated by the household at the point of consumption. I therefore aim at “completing” the MFA to include the household questionnaire in our cross-referencing of the data. -->

### Implications for research and policy

**Methodological implications**

The differences in overall inefficiency, as well as in the distribution of inefficiency across value chain stages and food groups observed in this chapter are, at least partly, artifacts of the survey design. This is because the original questionnaire shows that survey questions were asked differently (*i.e.* inconsistently) for different food groups and stages. This implies not only a need to harmonise questions across food groups and stages, but more fundamentally to embed cross-referencing as a principle of survey design — analogous to the consistency checks already built into the LSMS-ISA. Extending this logic could involve separating respondents and fieldworkers across sections, allowing the same flow to be reported independently from both ends and the truth to be narrowed through triangulation. Where recall data are unreliable, objective measures such as GPS-verified plot sizes or biomarker-based intake estimates already serve as external anchors. This implies a need to harmonise survey instruments so that all food groups are treated with equal granularity — not only where surveys are repurposed for waste analysis, but as a general design principle. The current questionnaire collects considerably more detail on staple crops than on animal-source foods, which reflects and reinforces the broader evidence gap identified in Chapter \@ref(chapter2). A more balanced approach would ensure that production, loss, and allocation questions are asked consistently across food groups, regardless of the downstream use of the data. Given the scarcity and patchiness of observational data on losses for animal-source foods in particular, extrapolation from other studies is currently unreliable, limiting robust comparisons across food groups. Additionally, observational and qualitative data are needed to inform the breadth of destinations currently not consistently covered across food groups in the survey beyond sales, consumption, gifts, and payments. Such information would be particularly meaningful for animal-source foods, as small consumption amounts can have a meaningful impact on nutrition and health outcomes [@Khonje.2024].

**Implications for data and policy**

For survey designers and data custodians such as the World Bank LSMS team, these patterns point to persistent gaps in the data landscape. For national policymakers relying on this evidence base, the findings highlight an unbalanced picture that continues to prioritise post-harvest losses and staple crops over other forms of inefficiency. For example, the survey captures in detail different storage structures and methods reminiscent of the interventions described by Stathers et al. (2020) [@Stathers.2020] and in Chapter \@ref(chapter2). However, not all forms of inefficiency could be easily quantified or independently verified, as illustrated by milk and eggs, where discrepancies between survey-based estimates and official statistics remained large and unresolved. Year-on-year and regional variability, due for example to disease outbreaks, climate risks or differences in infrastructure, further complicates interpretation and calls for closer repeated measurements rather than surveys with five-year gaps.

Beyond data design, the findings carry direct implications for the prioritisation of waste-reduction policies. The quantitative results from this chapter indicate that waste-prevention policies should prioritise nutrient-dense foods—such as fruits, vegetables, and animal-source products—for health and environmental reasons, even though they usually require more integrated waste-reduction approaches than staple crops. These more complex interventions are likely to offer more direct and immediate benefits at the household level, for example, through improved diet quality and income, which will be discussed in more detail in Chapter \@ref(chapter4). Across the sample there was only a small number of producers of vegetables, which has been confirmed by the most recent official NBS statistics (the first report to include vegetables specifically) [@NBS.2025]. Average post-harvest losses were highest for vegetable production, possibly reflecting a higher risk of production, handling, and cost-inefficiency more broadly [@Makule.2024; @Mboghoina.2024]. Policies that reduce this risk — through improved access to short cold chains, local processing capacity, and market linkages — could both lower losses and incentivise a wider range of smallholders to produce vegetables, with direct nutritional and income benefits.

### Positioning and reflexivity

Growing up on a fruit farm, I witnessed a region-wide shift from diverse production to specialised systems to mitigate risk. My childhood was spent listening to—and implicitly participating in—constant reflections on what to produce, how to produce it, where to sell it and how to adjust when plans fail. I observed how producers constantly judge their own and others’ decisions and practices, exercising agency that fundamentally shapes the landscape and the food system around them.

This early exposure to the actor's internal logic informed my systemic approach to this chapter. I wanted to see if this agency—the calculated decisions about destinations and risk-aversion—could be uncovered in the data, assuming it was a universal approach. Under this view, every production decision becomes a manifestation of waste prevention or mitigation behaviour (as explored further in Chapter \@ref(chapter6)).

This systemic approach eventually displaced my original intent to model nutrient flows. While my background as a nutritionist made me curious about uncovering the meaningful nutritional impact of waste, I chose to stop this endeavour. Nutrient content is too unreliable an estimate—dependent on soil, species, and storage conditions—and I decided not to over-infer from 'shaky' data. I chose a less exciting mass-balance result to maintain scientific integrity and avoid adding to the rhetorical over-certainty I had criticised in the literature.

At first glance, this study may appear to be yet another quantitative study using secondary data—something I initially wanted to avoid in favour of primary data collection. However, the constraints of the pandemic necessitated a methodological pivot. This shift ultimately highlighted the potential yet shaky nature of microdata (including LSMS-ISA) currently informing policy. This chapter, therefore, does more than quantifying waste; it exposes the bias in our day-to-day tools — the surveys, questionnaires and data collection protocols through which we construct knowledge about food systems. It illustrates how the data we collect shapes what we 'know', and conversely how our limited understanding of waste practices continues to shape the inadequate data we collect.

## Conclusion

In this chapter, we have provided an estimate of quantities of food flows across a nationally representative sample of smallholder farm households in the Republic of Tanzania. Total annual production across all food groups (except fish), and the subsequent allocation of these foods to processing, sales, household consumption and others were quantified. In terms of inefficacies, we were able to highlight multiple points in the system from pre-harvest to allocation to sales and household consumption. Nonetheless, studies have shown that not all food allocated to households is fully consumed. The next chapter therefore extends the MFA boundary to quantify inefficiencies that occur within the household stage of the food value chain.
